mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensively covers German conquest of Poland and France, the Battle of Britain, war in North Africa (B.1), the Pacific war including Pearl Harbor and Japanese conquests (B.2), the Allied counteroffensive in Europe (B.3), Axis Powers' strategy (B.4), Allied diplomacy including the Atlantic Charter (B.5), and military technology developments (B.6).

WWI's peace settlements and the Treaty of Versailles established the post-war diplomatic framework including the League of Nations, US and Soviet isolation, and crises over enforcement of the peace settlement (A.1.b).

The Cold War directly covers the Iron Curtain and Soviet consolidation of power in eastern Europe (C.2.c.i), Cold War as ideology and Realpolitik (C.2.c.ii), the Berlin blockade and confrontation in Germany (C.2.c.iii), NATO formation (C.2.c.iv), the continuing Cold War including containment, de-Stalinization, and nuclear balance of terror (C.2.f), and postwar economic growth and superpower dynamics (C.3.a).
The British Empire's colonial territories, client states, and demands for self-determination (A.3.c), colonial territories of Asia (C.2.d), and decolonization (C.3.a.ii).

Stalin's establishment of Nazi-era economic recovery based on War Communism and the New Economic Policy (A.4.c), the German-Soviet Nonaggression Pact and subsequent German invasion (B.1), the Nazi-Soviet pact ideology (C.1.b.i), and Soviet expansion and postwar Eastern Bloc formation (C.2.c).

Russia's Soviet-era history covers consolidation of power in eastern Europe (C.2.c), de-Stalinization and Khrushchev (C.2.f.iv), and the disintegration of the Soviet Union and end of the Cold War (C.3.a.x).
German conquest of Poland (1939) opening World War II is directly covered (B.1), and Poland's post-war communist rule and Solidarity's role connect to Eastern Bloc dynamics.
NATO's formation to counter Soviet expansion (C.2.c.iv) and NATO's role in European economic recovery (C.2.f.vi) are directly covered.
The Korean War's meaning, course, and consequences are directly covered (C.2.d.iii), including US and UN intervention.
The Spanish Civil War (1936–39) and the failure of diplomatic attempts to avert broader war are directly covered (A.3.b).

The US role in post-war international order covers the atomic bomb and Truman's principles (C.2.a), containment policy and McCarthyism (C.2.f.i–iii), and postwar US economic dominance and Cold War dynamics (C.3.a).
Hitler's Nazi Germany and rearmament-based economic recovery (A.4.c), Germany's conquest of Poland and France opening WWII (B.1), and the Nazi-Soviet pact (C.1.b.i) are directly covered.
Italian aggression against Ethiopia (1935–36) and failure of the League of Nations to check it are directly covered under international affairs in the 1930s (A.3.b).

Europe's history covering two World Wars, Cold War division, and post-1989 integration broadly maps to the interwar period (A), World War II (B), and peace and integration movements in postwar Europe (C.3.b).
Mao's leadership in civil conflict in China (A.3.a) and communist rule following civil war in China (C.2.d.ii) are directly covered.
The economic and political impact of the Great Depression of the 1930s — collapse of the world market and government responses — is directly covered (A.4.b).
The Iranian case in the postwar Middle East (C.2.e.ii) and the Iran-Iraq War and Persian Gulf wars (C.3.c.ii) are directly covered.
The transformation of the American role in Southeast Asia (C.2.d.iv) and the Indochina War (C.3.c.i) directly cover the Vietnam conflict.
France as a nuclear power is directly covered in the problem of nuclear energy and the balance of terror (C.2.f.viii).
Nasser and the Suez crisis are directly covered (C.2.e.iv) as part of the Middle East in the postwar period.
The Turkish case in the postwar Middle East is directly covered (C.2.e.ii).

Nazi Germany's economic recovery through rearmament (A.4.c), the Berlin blockade and confrontation in Germany (C.2.c.iii), and the resolution of the German problem including the Berlin Wall, reunification, and Helsinki conference (C.3.a.x).
Algeria's independence struggle and civil war connect to sub-Saharan Africa's superpower involvement and civil warfare (C.3.c.iii) and decolonization more broadly.
Italian aggression against Ethiopia (1935–36) is covered under the 1930s international affairs (A.3.b), and post-colonial African conflicts connect to sub-Saharan Africa (C.3.c.iii).
Myanmar's ongoing civil war connects to Southeast Asian conflict areas (C.3.c.i).
Civil conflict in China and Japanese seizure of Manchuria (A.3.a), civil war and communist rule (C.2.d.ii), and Sino-Soviet relations and their rift (C.3.a.viii) are covered.
Churchill's role in WWII and the Atlantic Charter (C.1.b.ii) connect to Allied wartime leadership.

War Communism and the New Economic Policy (A.4.c), Soviet consolidation of power in Eastern Europe (C.2.c), de-Stalinization (C.2.f.iv), Soviet 'national liberation' support (C.3.a.iii), and the dissolution of the Soviet Union (C.3.a.x).
Decolonization as a process directly maps to the post-WWII decolonization (C.3.a.ii) and the new states' nonalignment and domestic problems (C.3.a.iv).
The Middle East in the postwar period (C.2.e) covering Palestine, Israel, Arabs, and the Suez crisis, and the Arab-Israeli wars, petroleum, and Persian Gulf wars (C.3.c.ii).
Nigeria's post-independence civil wars and military dictatorships connect to sub-Saharan Africa's civil warfare and ethnic strife (C.3.c.iii).
Palestine, Israel, and the Arabs (C.2.e.iii) and the Arab-Israeli wars (C.3.c.ii) are directly covered.
Singapore's post-independence economic transformation connects to the new states' emergence and development following decolonization (C.3.a.iv).
European history from the interwar period through WWII and Cold War integration provides broad context for the period between wars (A), World War II (B), and peace in postwar Europe (C.3.b).
North American history covering the postwar boom and Vietnam War era connects to Latin America in the Cold War and Castro (C.3.a.vi).
European colonial empires and demands for self-determination (A.3.c) and post-WWII decolonization (C.3.a.ii) are directly covered.
